title: Level 134
wikitext:
{{DISPLAYTITLE:Level 134: N/A}}
{{VoidedTheme|mono}} {{Background|file=void.png}}__NOTOC__ {{Quote|Often what is not has a greater sway on the human psyche than what is|Unknown, January 9, 1998}} ==Description== '''Level 134''' is an unknown level existing between levels [[Level 133|133]] and [[Level 135|135]]. While there have been no confirmed cases of wanderers entering the level, its existence has been proven by a coherent noise resembling howling wind [[Phenomenon 13|produced by radios]] when picking up sounds from [[Object 2|Level Key]] at a 134 MHz frequency, the discovery of naturally generated [[Phenomenon 2|BACK-NET]] pages hosted on 134All pages hosted at Level 134 have consisted of either blank space or nonsensical patterns and spaces, as well as by mathematical calculations based on the relative position of levels to each other in [[The Void]]. ==History== By the time the Casper-Bray Numbering System began to become widely adopted in the 1980s, most levels numbered from 0 to the 150s had already been discovered and readily documented. While there were several gaps in the record, the general assumption was that most missing levels were simply undiscovered. This was supported in 1986, when [[Level 126]], which had long been elusive, was discovered, and with the similarly enigmatic levels [[Level 110|110]], [[Level 101|101]], [[Level 98|98]] and the last sub-100, [[Level 64|64]] also being found by the end of the decade. The first reference to Level 134 being inaccesable was found on a [[Backchat (Collaborative Log)|Backchat]] thread concerning a hypothetical pattern in the properties of levels based on their numbering, stating that all levels ending in 34 can only be entered by entering a certain object in any levelAt the time, the only levels ending in 34 known were [[Level 34]] and [[Level 334]], which fit that pattern., and that Level 134 corresponded to an object that was not yet known by humanity and thus didn't exist in any other levels, explaining its inaccessibility. By the early 1990s, a small but dedicated online community had formed around hopes of discovering Level 134. This group, which called themselves the 134 Club or the Seekers, mostly consisted of wanderers from settlements in levels nearby Level 134, such as those in [[Level 131]], [[Level 124]] and [[Level 112]], hoping to find fame with the discovery of the nearby level. The Seekers's most notable moment was their attempt at an ill-fated expedition to [[Level 133]] in an attempt to find an entrance to the level they have been searching for, as many levels have been known to contain an enterance to the one numerically following them. Unfortunately, the group went missing, and presumably froze to death due to the levels' harsh climate. This may have served as an end to the search for Level 134, if it were not swept up in a cultural phenomenon making its way through the Backrooms. The Liminal Renaissance or Backrooms Identity movement was a sociocultural movement that promoted unity among the peoples of the Backrooms, and to emphasize the culture of communities in the Backrooms over those inherited from [[The Frontrooms]]. Many authors and artists in this movement created works inspired by the experiences of life in The Backrooms. Inspired by the tragic fate of the 134 Club's expedition, Evan Thomas, an acclaimed authorAlthough his reputation has been tarnished in recent years due to his association with Backborn supremacist groups, wrote the book ''Search For a Better Place,'' describing a dramatized account of the expedition. While this alone was not enough to bring Level 134 into the public eye, ''Search For a Better Place'' served as an inspiration for the widely popular ''Behind The Door'' series of novelsAlso credited with popularizing the term "Backrooms" over the more antiquated "Wake", with the fourth book in the series, ''Lost at Dawn'', featuring a plot line where one of the characters succeeds in entering Level 134, with the book never clarifying what lies in the level other than it being "beautiful". A video adaptation of the series created by a collaboration of [[Level 40]]'s Rip-Off Movie Collective and an [[Republic of Aurielle|Auriellen]] filmmaking group, depicting Level 134 as a flat, crystalline plain with warped geometry, only further increased the level's popularityEspecially due to the low literacy rate in many parts of the Backrooms.. Due to these developments, interest in Level 134 exploded into the mainstream, with wanderers across the Backrooms being entranced by the tragedy and mystique surrounding the level. A recent analysis shows that mentions of Level 134 on Backchat increased by over 1300% over just 2 months from January 1996 to March 1996. A [[Backrooms Today]] article released in April of that year described the phenomenon as 134-Mania, a term which became widely used to describe it. Many more works were created imagining hypothetical versions of Level 134, ranging from an endless brick pathway above an endless beach, a infinite library filled with madness-inducing knowledge, a mental asylum similar to [[Level 16]], or even something as mundane as a Japanese restaurant. Due to advice of the [[The Kauer Research Organization|Kauer Research Organization]], these works were required to put clear disclaimers of their fictionality, to prevent any incidents of [[Phenomenon 9|Tulpaefictation]]. The period of 134-Mania led to the start of additional attempts to discover the level. Due to the now well-known environmental dangers in Level 133, leading to the main targets of expeditions being [[Level 132]], [[Level 135]], and [[Level 136]]. [[Level 131]], which was known to exist but was not officially documented or settled until decades later was targeted to a lesser degree. After several expeditions had resulted in failure to discover Level 134 and the deaths or disappearances of their membersDespite this, the wave of expeditions was not entirely fruitless, as one was credited as leading to the discovery of [[Level 197]], multiple groups, including the [[M.E.G.]], [[Backroom Colonists]], and the [[Republic of Level 153]] banned their members from organizing or supplying any attempt to locate Level 134 if it involved entering a potentially dangerous level. After it became clear that looking in nearby levels was not going to work, wanderers began to attempt more creative ways of entering the level. It had already become known that despite Level 134 having a corresponding door in [[The Hub]], no [[Object 2|Level Keys]] have been found for this door, and attempts to break it down have proved unsuccessful. Additionally, neither [[Level 154]] nor [[The Metro]] had Level 134 as a stop. This led some wanderers to propose using exits that would send one to a random level repeatedly, but even the 134 fanatics saw this as a foolish idea. Others attempted to use [https://backrooms.fandom.com/wiki/No-Clipping?redirect=no Meditative Noclipping], focusing on the howling sounds produced by Level Key-attuned radios, with it turning out that a sound alone was not enough to locate a specific level. Attempts at [[Phenomenon 1-A|Linear Noclipping]] have also failed, with wanderers attempting a linear noclip to Level 134 suddenly being stopped in Level 133. During this rush to discover Level 134, there were several claims of an alleged discovery of the level, all of which have since been proven false, most being hoaxes or instances of [[Phenomenon 99|Temporary Manifestation]]. One notable instance was the discovery of [[Level 135.1]] in 1998, which was erroneously reported as being Level 134 due to a faulty radio being used for its Level Key frequency identificationThis false discovery was reported by Backrooms Today despite the dubious nature of the claim, leading to some claiming the group was beginning to value sensationalism over accuracy in its reporting.. Eventually, as more and more attempts to enter the level came back as a failure and were condemned by more and more groups, the 134-Mania began to cool down just before the turn of the century. A brief resurgence of interest occurred in 2002, when [[Level 116]] was discovered, making Level 134 the only undiscovered level with a numbering between 100 to 150, a title which it retains today. While not as frequently used as in its peak of popularity, Level 134 seems to still pervade the public imagination, occasionally featuring in both works of fiction and nonfiction, especially in content made to evoke nostalgia for the Liminal Renaissance of the 90s. For example, the popular satiric webcomic ''Yes-Clipping'' featured a flashback to 1997 in which the protagonist's father attempts to ingest a mixture of [[Object 44|Cashew Water]] and [[Object 12|Liquid Pain]], as he believes it would transport him to Level 134. ==Ongoing Research and Theories== Since the 1990s, scientists from various groups have formulated theories on the reason for Level 134's inaccessibility. === Null Theory === Null Theory states that the reason Level 134 cannot be accessed is that it was erased from reality by unknown means some time before its discovery. They compare its properties to other levels such as [[[Null|ERROR: NULL]]] which have been known to be erased, such as an absence of Level Keys and a lack of entrances and exits. While this was at one point the most popular theory regarding Level 134, it has since been discredited due to key differences between Level 134 and erased levels, such as Level 134's frequency not being silent, and Linear Noclipping attempts resulting in wanderers being stopped rather than sent to [[The Void]]. Despite this, Null Theory still has some supporters, with various explanations for the discrepancies such as the level not being fully erased being brought up in modern versions of it. === Isolation Theory === The Isolation Theory speculates that some force, such as a powerful entity or secretive group, is purposely blocking access to Level 134. This theory began after it was shown that Linear Noclipping is unsuccessful in entering Level 134, making it seem as if something is purposefully blocking entrances to the level. While the lack of evidence has made it so there has been little serious consideration of this theory, it is popular in fiction about Level 134 due to it allowing for a variety of stories, and commonly features in conspiracy theories alleging nefarious actions by various groups, such as those propagated on the [[Extended BACK-NET Website List|Free Backrooms Forum]]. === Tulpaeic Lock Theory === The Tulpaeic Lock Theory is a recently formulated theory on Level 134's absence. A variation on the Isolation theory, the Tulpaeic Lock Theory states that while Level 134 originally was simply difficult to access, the combined belief that it was inaccessible or incredibly difficult to access created a Tulpa which blocked off the level's enterances and stopped any form of entrance into the level, explaining why methods around the Backrooms such as use of Linear Noclipping or use of The Hub do not function. While the theory does not have much evidence directly supporting it, it is consistent with other occurrences of psychic phenomena in The Backrooms. First proposed in Anna Yung's 2017 book ''Searching For a Level That Does Not Exist'', the theory has become one of the most prominent in the modern day. {{Collapse|initial='''> Open Author & Licensing Information''' |final='''> Close Author & Licensing Information''' |body=
Written and designed by [[User:CanineAnnoyer|CanineAnnoyer]].
}}
